title: Temptation in the Wilderness
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: 01-29-23 - temption in the Wilderness - Matthew 4_1-11 - Pastor Tito Lyro.txt

outline.title: Introduction: The Context of the Temptation
subpoints: Immediately following His baptism, the Spirit leads Jesus into the wilderness
Jesus is led by the Spirit — this is not an accident but a divine appointment
40 days: parallels Moses (Exodus 34:28), Elijah (1 Kings 19:8), and Israel (40 years in the wilderness)

outline.title: 1. Jesus as Second Adam and Second Israel
subpoints: Adam was tempted in a garden of abundance and fell; Jesus is tempted in a wilderness of deprivation and stands
Israel was tempted in the wilderness for 40 years and repeatedly failed; Jesus succeeds where Israel failed
Matthew's structure: Jesus relives Israel's history — out of Egypt, through the waters, into the wilderness — and does it perfectly

outline.title: 2. The First Temptation: Stones to Bread (Matthew 4:3-4)
subpoints: Satan attacks at the point of physical need after 40 days of fasting
'If you are the Son of God, command these stones to become bread'
Jesus answers: 'Man shall not live by bread alone, but by every word that proceeds from the mouth of God' (Deut. 8:3)
Israel grumbled about food in the wilderness; Jesus trusts the Father's provision
The temptation: use divine power for personal comfort outside the Father's will

outline.title: 3. The Second Temptation: The Pinnacle of the Temple (Matthew 4:5-7)
subpoints: Satan quotes Psalm 91 — he can use Scripture; the question is whether it is used rightly
'If you are the Son of God, throw yourself down, for the angels will catch you'
Jesus answers: 'You shall not put the Lord your God to the test' (Deut. 6:16)
Israel tested God at Massah (Exodus 17); Jesus refuses to demand a demonstration of God's faithfulness
The temptation: force God to prove His promises by staging a miraculous rescue

outline.title: 4. The Third Temptation: All the Kingdoms (Matthew 4:8-10)
subpoints: Satan offers Jesus all the kingdoms of the world — at the price of worship
The offer is real: Satan does have temporary authority over the kingdoms of this world
Jesus answers: 'You shall worship the Lord your God, and Him only you shall serve' (Deut. 6:13)
Israel worshipped the golden calf rather than wait for God's timing; Jesus refuses a shortcut to the crown
The temptation: receive the inheritance without the cross

outline.title: 5. Application: We Need Not Fall Because Christ Did Not Fall
subpoints: Hebrews 4:15: 'We do not have a high priest who is unable to sympathize with our weaknesses, but one who in every respect has been tempted as we are, yet without sin'
Christ's victory is not merely an example — it is our ground of confidence before God
Union with Christ means His righteousness is counted as ours; His victory over temptation is ours
When you are tempted: remember you have a High Priest who overcame for you; flee to Him

summary: Pastor Tito Lyro preaches on the temptation of Jesus in Matthew 4:1-11, presenting Christ as the second Adam and second Israel who succeeded where both failed. He traces the typological significance of the 40-day fast and expounds each of the three temptations — bread from stones, the temple pinnacle, and the offer of the kingdoms — noting that Jesus answered each with a quotation from Deuteronomy 6-8, the passage Israel received after their wilderness failure. He closes with Hebrews 4:15, applying Christ's sinless endurance of temptation as the ground of the believer's confidence and the source of help when tempted.
transcript:
## Introduction: Led Into the Wilderness

> "Then Jesus was led up by the Spirit into the wilderness to be tempted by the devil. And after fasting forty days and forty nights, he was hungry." (Matthew 4:1–2)

Notice: the Spirit *led* Jesus into the wilderness. This is not a detour. It is not a setback. It is a divine appointment. Jesus enters the wilderness because the Father has ordained that He face what He is about to face.

The forty days immediately recalls three figures from the Old Testament: Moses, who fasted forty days and nights on the mountain of God (Exodus 34:28); Elijah, who journeyed forty days to the mountain of God (1 Kings 19:8); and Israel, who wandered forty years in the wilderness before entering the Promised Land.

This last connection is the most important for Matthew's Gospel.

## Jesus as Second Adam and Second Israel

Matthew carefully structures his account of Jesus' early life to echo Israel's history. Jesus is taken to Egypt and called back — echoing the Exodus. He passes through the waters of baptism — echoing the Red Sea crossing. He enters the wilderness — echoing Israel's forty years.

But there is an even deeper echo. Adam was tempted in a garden — in abundance, in beauty, with every provision. He fell. Israel was tested in the wilderness — sustained by God, given His law, shown His glory. They fell repeatedly.

Jesus is tempted in the wilderness — in deprivation, in weakness, with nothing. He stands.

This is the heart of what Matthew wants us to see: the one whom Adam and Israel could not be, Jesus is. He is the faithful Son, the true Israel, the second Adam who undoes what the first Adam did.

## The First Temptation: Bread (Matthew 4:3–4)

> "The tempter came to him and said, 'If you are the Son of God, command these stones to become bread.'" (Matthew 4:3)

Satan attacks at the point of physical weakness — forty days of fasting, genuine hunger. And notice the form of the temptation: *if you are the Son of God*. The question is not really about bread. It is about identity. The Father just declared at Jesus' baptism, 'This is my beloved Son.' Satan says: prove it.

The temptation is to use divine power for personal comfort, outside the Father's explicit will and timing.

Jesus answers from Deuteronomy 8:3:

> "Man shall not live by bread alone, but by every word that proceeds from the mouth of God." (Matthew 4:4)

In Deuteronomy 8, Moses reminds Israel of how God humbled them and fed them with manna — to teach them that man does not live by bread alone but by the word of God. Israel grumbled. Israel despaired. Jesus trusts the Father to provide in His own way and His own time.

## The Second Temptation: The Pinnacle (Matthew 4:5–7)

> "Then the devil took him to the holy city and set him on the pinnacle of the temple and said to him, 'If you are the Son of God, throw yourself down, for it is written, "He will command his angels concerning you," and "On their hands they will bear you up, lest you strike your foot against a stone."'" (Matthew 4:5–6)

Now Satan quotes Scripture — Psalm 91:11–12. This should alert us: the ability to cite the Bible does not indicate fidelity to God. The question is whether Scripture is being used rightly.

Satan is telling Jesus to force God's hand — to create a situation where God must intervene miraculously to keep His promise. This is the temptation to test God, to demand a sign of His faithfulness.

Israel did exactly this at Massah: 'Is the LORD among us or not?' (Exodus 17:7). They demanded proof.

Jesus answers from Deuteronomy 6:16:

> "You shall not put the Lord your God to the test." (Matthew 4:7)

Faith does not need God to perform tricks to prove Himself. Faith rests on who God has revealed Himself to be.

## The Third Temptation: The Kingdoms (Matthew 4:8–10)

> "Again, the devil took him to a very high mountain and showed him all the kingdoms of the world and their glory. And he said to him, 'All these I will give you, if you will fall down and worship me.'" (Matthew 4:8–9)

The offer is real. Satan does hold temporary authority over the kingdoms of this world — he is 'the ruler of this world' (John 12:31). He is offering Jesus what is rightfully His — but by a different path than the Father has ordained.

The inheritance without the cross. The crown without the suffering. This is the deepest temptation.

Israel chose the golden calf rather than wait for Moses to return with God's law. They wanted the blessing on their own terms and timeline.

Jesus answers from Deuteronomy 6:13:

> "You shall worship the Lord your God and him only shall you serve." (Matthew 4:10)

The kingdoms will be His — but through the cross, through resurrection, through the Father's way and the Father's timing.

## Application: We Need Not Fall

> "For we do not have a high priest who is unable to sympathize with our weaknesses, but one who in every respect has been tempted as we are, yet without sin. Let us then with confidence draw near to the throne of grace, that we may receive mercy and find grace to help in time of need." (Hebrews 4:15–16)

Christ's victory is not merely an inspiring example. He is your High Priest. His righteousness is counted as yours. His victory over temptation is the ground on which you stand before God.

When temptation comes — and it will — remember that you have a Savior who was tempted in every way as you are, and did not fall. Flee to Him. Cast yourself on His mercy. And remember: you do not have to fall. Not because you are strong enough, but because He was.
